coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots with high quantum yield for the detection of Fe3+ and antibacterial property

coredata.dc:description: The practical utility of traditional carbon dots (CDs) is often limited by their relatively low quantum yield and suboptimal energy conversion capabilities. In this work, we synthesized nitrogen-doped carbonized polymer dots (N-CPDs) with high quantum yields reached by 72 % using citric acid (CA) and low molecular polyethylenimide (PEI). The optimized N-CPDs exhibited remarkable photobleaching resistance and long-term colloidal stability. Additionally, N-CPDs demonstrated superior quenching effects on Fe³⁺ than other metal ions with a high selectivity in the concentration range of 1–100 μmol·L⁻¹. Further investigation into the formation mechanism suggested that N-CPDs were generated through a process of dehydration condensation, cross-linked polymerization, and carbonization, as confirmed by various spectroscopic analyses. In vitro antibacterial tests, N-CPDs synthesized achieved a high antibacterial rate of up to 98 % against both Gram-positive and negative bacteria. Meanwhile, cytotoxicity assays confirmed the low biotoxicity of N-CPDs by an almost indistinguishable rate of healing comparable to the control group. The high antibacterial activity combined with low biotoxicity opens new avenues for applications in medical fields, such as in coatings for medical devices and wound dressings.

(a) The fluorescence spectra of N-CPDs at different λex from 280 to 430 nm and fluorescence spectra at PH (1–14) (b). The effects of long-time storage in solid state (black) and in solution (red) (c), xenon lamp irradiation (d) of N-CPDs.
Fluorescence spectra of aqueous solutions with concentrations of 1 (a), 3 (b), 5 (c), 7 (d) and 10 (e) mg/ml of N-CPDs and normalized maximum emission peak fluorescence spectra (e).
TEM images of the products at different reaction stages: the temperature reached 120 ℃ (a), 140 ℃ (b), 160 ℃ (c), 180 ℃ (d) and maintained at 180 ℃ for 1 h (e), 2 h (f), 3 h (g), 4 h (h) and 5 h (I).

(e, g) Ros levels in S. aureus (e) and E. coli (g) after the treatment of N-CPDs. (f, g) statistical analysis of ROS levels in S. aureus (f) and E. coli (h) after the treatment of N-CPDs.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots with high quantum yield for the detection of Fe3+ and antibacterial property

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tianjin University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The practical utility of traditional carbon dots (CDs) is often limited by their relatively low quantum yield and suboptimal energy conversion capabilities. In this work, we synthesized nitrogen-doped carbonized polymer dots (N-CPDs) with high quantum yields reached by 72 % using citric acid (CA) and low molecular polyethylenimide (PEI). The optimized N-CPDs exhibited remarkable photobleaching resistance and long-term colloidal stability. Additionally, N-CPDs demonstrated superior quenching effects on Fe³⁺ than other metal ions with a high selectivity in the concentration range of 1–100 μmol·L⁻¹. Further investigation into the formation mechanism suggested that N-CPDs were generated through a process of dehydration condensation, cross-linked polymerization, and carbonization, as confirmed by various spectroscopic analyses. In vitro antibacterial tests, N-CPDs synthesized achieved a high antibacterial rate of up to 98 % against both Gram-positive and negative bacteria. Meanwhile, cytotoxicity assays confirmed the low biotoxicity of N-CPDs by an almost indistinguishable rate of healing comparable to the control group. The high antibacterial activity combined with low biotoxicity opens new avenues for applications in medical fields, such as in coatings for medical devices and wound dressings.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) represent a significant complement to the emerging field of nanomaterial. Their potential properties have been extensively investigated, revealing excellent optical characteristics, high solubility, favorable biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity further supporting extensive research in various fields, including optoelectronic devices [1,2], fluorescent sensors [3,4], biological imaging [5,6], and intracellular detection techniques [7–10]. The synthetic routes of CDs primarily follows two strategies: top-down and bottom-up. The top-down methods are advantageous in terms of large-scale and abundant raw materials, but the sizes of the synthesized CDs may be non-uniform and require further separation [11]. In contrast, bottom-up routes enable precise control over CDs' size, morphology, and surface functionalization, thereby facilitating the modulation of their physicochemical and photoluminescent properties [12]. This tunability underscores the critical role of synthesis methodologies in tailoring CDs for targeted applications.

A key determinant of CDs optical performance lies in their surface chemistry. The optical properties of CDs usually come from surface modification and passivation of carbon by CDs with heteroatoms such as nitrogen, oxygen, and sulfur, which produce surface defects producing photoluminescence properties make them ideal candidates for a wide range of applications [13,14]. However, despite these advances, the quantum yield (QY) of most CDs remains suboptimal compared to conventional semiconductor quantum dots, limiting their energy conversion efficiency and practical utility in high-precision applications [15]. Furthermore, a large number of studies has focused on the synthetic methods and exploiting CDs singular functionalities, such as fluorescent probes or bioimaging agents. Nonetheless, the multifunctionality of CDs has not been fully utilized. CDs have demonstrated considerable promise in a variety of biomedical applications, including drug delivery, imaging, and treatment, as evidenced by substantial research outcomes [16]. With the significant advancements witnessed in the domain of biomedicine, research exploring the antibacterial properties of CDs remains in great significance. New requirements for antibacterial materials are constantly put forward establishing them as a mature and promising class of antimicrobial nanomaterials [17,18]. Their potential in antimicrobial therapy, a field demanding materials with synergistic biocompatibility and bactericidal efficacy, has yet to be systematically harnessed. The realization of this potential would significantly expand the practical applications of the CDs.

The emergence of multidrug-resistant pathogens has led to an increased demand for novel antimicrobial agents. While nanomaterials, such as silver nanoparticles and metal oxides, have been shown to possess potent antibacterial properties, their inherent toxicity and environmental persistence impose substantial limitations. CDs have emerged as a promising alternative for 0-dimensional nanomaterials. Their nanoscale dimensions (< 10 nm) and high surface-to-volume ratio enhance interactions with microbial membranes, enabling mechanisms such as membrane disruption, reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, and metabolic interference [19]. These advantages contribute to the significant antibacterial activity of CDs, which includes the inhibition of bacterial growth, the disruption of cell membrane structure, and the induction of cell death, particularly in drug-resistant strains [20]. The interaction between the dopant and the carbon source can result in alterations to the nanostructures and surface properties of the elements introduced. These alterations determine whether toxicity will be introduced and whether the elements will be environmentally friendly [21]. In light of these findings, researchers are continually refining the synthesis methods and modification strategies of CDs to enhance their antibacterial properties and biocompatibility [22,23].

In this work, we report a facile hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbonized polymer dots (N-CPDs) using citric acid as the carbon precursor and polyethyleneimine (PEI) as a passivating agent [24,25]. This one-pot approach combines dehydration, condensation, and carbonization processes to achieve uniform N-CPDs with tunable surface defects. Enough surface defects were introduced through the abundant functional groups of low molecular weight polymers, we enhanced fluorescent properties, rendering them ideal for use as fluorescent probes for detecting Fe3+ with high accuracy and precision. Simultaneously, the tailored surface chemistry endows N-CPDs with robust antibacterial activity against Gram-positive and Gram-negative pathogens. Through systematic characterization of intermediate stages, we elucidate the formation mechanism and structure-property relationships of N-CPDs. This dual functionality, precision sensing and antimicrobial action, addresses the existing gap in multifunctional CDs design, offering a sustainable platform for applications in environmental monitoring and infection-resistant biomedical devices.

Citric acid (CA) was purchased from Tianjin Sciens Biochemical Technology Corporation. Polyethyleneimine (PEI) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Aladdin. Fe3+ and Fe2+ were prepared from ferric chloride FeCl3 and ferrous chloride FeCl2, and the solutions of other metal ions were prepared from their nitrate, acetate, or chloride salts and were freshly prepared before use. The above reagents are of analytical grade and are used directly without further purification. Ultrapure water was used in all experiments.

CA (3 g) and PEI (1 g) were added to a beaker containing 50 mL of ultrapure water and sonicated at 500 W until complete dissolution. The mixture was then transferred to a 100 mL hydrothermal reactor with a polytetrafluoroethylene liner and heated at 180 °C for 3 h. The solution changed from clear to dark yellow, indicating successful synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbonized polymer dots (N-CPDs). After the solution was cooled to room temperature, it was centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 20 min to remove the larger particles and the supernatant was retained for further purification. The above centrifuged solution was dialyzed with a 500 Da dialysis bag for 24 h, and the water was changed every 4 h to obtain a yellow transparent solution. The solution was stored in the refrigerator at 5 °C. Finally, the yellow solution was freeze-dried to obtain N-CPDs powder, which was stored away from light for further applications.

Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM): The N-CPDs powder was prepared into a solution of 0.1 mg/mL and ultrasonically treated for 15 min to ensure uniform dispersion. It is then dripped onto an ultra-thin carbon film, naturally dried at room temperature, and tested at a voltage of 200KV. UV-Vis: Prepare the N-CPDs powder into a 0.1 mg/mL solution in 1 cm quartz cuvette, with a scanning speed of 1 nm, and record the spectra at 220–800 nm. Fluorescence spectrophotometer： A stock solution of N-CPDs was prepared at 1 mg mL⁻¹ in ultrapure water then diluted to 0.1 mg mL⁻¹ for all optical measurements with emission collected from 380 to 600 nm under identical instrument settings. Set the parameters to 2.5EM and 2.5EX, and set the excitation voltage to 700 eV. X-ray diffraction test (XRD): The prepared carbon dots were ground and screened to 500 mesh. A portion was placed on a glass dish for XRD testing. The test range was set at 10–60°, and the scanning speed was 8°·min-1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS): Ground N-CPDs is placed on a non-conductive adhesive and pressurized with a pressure device to obtain a standard sample that is easy to measure. Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) Approximately 2 mg of N-CPDs was mixed with 200 mg of spectroscopic-grade KBr (sample:KBr ≈ 1:100 by mass), ground to a fine powder (< 2 µm), and pressed into a transparent pellet for 5 min under vacuum. Scan in diffuse reflectance mode from 4000 to 500 cm⁻¹. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR): Prepare 1 mg/mL of the sample, use deionized water or methanol as the solvent (different systems are used for different items), and ultrasonically disperse for 1 min. Take 30 μl of the sample, add 30 μl of DMPO (with 100 mM deionized water as the solvent), mix evenly, then draw a certain amount of the mixed solution with a capillary tube, place it in a quartz tube, and put it into the EPR resonant cavity for the test of hydroxyl radicals. Take 20 μl of bacteria with an absorbance of 0.7, and conduct SEM scanning test after dehydration with 10 %, 30 %, 50 %, 70 %, and 100 % alcohol.

Rhodamine 6 G was used as the standard reference with known quantum yield of 92 %. The absorbance of the standard and test solutions is controlled between 0.08 and 0.07 to avoid measurement errors caused by too high or too low absorbance. The equation is:

Φst refers to the known quantum yield of the standard solution. Fx and Fst correspond to the fluorescence integral intensities of sample and reference. fst and fx refers the absorbance factors of sample and reference. nx and nst are the refractive indices of sample and reference.

Photo bleaching resistance: take the N-CPDs aqueous solution with a concentration of 0.1 mg/mL and continuously irradiate it under a 450 W xenon lamp for 120 min and a fluorescence spectrophotometer with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm and the main peak emission intensity detected is used to record the curve of fluorescence intensity with irradiation record fluorescence spectra every 20 min. PH stability: prepare an N-CPDs solution with a concentration of 0.1 mg/mL at PH 1–14, and then measure the fluorescence intensity at 360 nm. Aging test: maintain in solid state and 0.1 mg/mL aqueous solution for 12 days respectively, and the fluorescence intensity was recorded every two days with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Concentration dependent assay: the prepared N-CPDs were made into aqueous solutions with different concentrations (e.g. 1, 2, 5, 7 and 10 mg/mL) for fluorescence spectra. Temperature stability: Stored at a constant temperature of 30, 50 and 70 ℃ for 40 min respectively with a concentration of 0.1 mg/mL, record fluorescence intensity every 5 min.Repeated freeze-thaw cycles: The samples were subjected to five alternating cycles at − 20 ℃ and room temperature, with each cycle lasting for 4 h. Record fluorescence intensity every cycle.

To test the fluorescence sensitivity of N-CPDs to various metal ions, including NaCl, KCl, ZnSO4·7H2O, MgCl2, Cl2Ni·6H2O, (NH4)2SO4, CaCl2, CuCl2·2H2O, CrCl3·6H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, FeCl2, MnCl2, BaCl2·2H2O, these metal chlorides and sulfates were freshly prepared by addition to ultrapure water. Typically, 0.1 mL of N-CPDs solution with a concentration of 1 mg/mL is mixed with 0.9 mL of one of the above metal salt solutions with a concentration of 1.1 μmol·L−1 to ensure that the final concentration of the metal salt solution is 1 μmol·L−1, and the mixture is ultrasonically treated for 5 min. The procedure for the determination of Fe3+ at room temperature is as follows. Configure Fe3+ solutions of different concentrations (0–100 μM) using the corresponding method. Record the fluorescence spectra in the range of 375–600 nm at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Set the excitation and emission slits to 2.5 nm. When conducting experiments on the Fe3+ solution, the concentration used was always 0.1 mg/mL.

Antibacterial studies were conducted using the spread plate method. Control group that no N-CPDs were added and experimental group that N-CPDs were added were set up. Bacteria (Gram positive bacteria: Staphylococcus aureus, Gram-negative bacteria: Escherichia coli) were cultured in broth to a concentration of 1 × 107 CFU/mL, and then 20 μL of the bacterial solution was mixed with different concentration gradients of the N-CPDs solution in a 96-well plate under aseptic conditions to ensure homogeneous mixing. Take 20 μL of the diluted bacterial solution and spread it on a Petri dish containing agar. Ensure that the spread is uniform throughout the process. The coated Petri dish is incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. At the end of the incubation, use the appropriate equipment to photograph and count the colonies on the plates.

For bacteria, the level of ROS was investigated using the oxidant-sensing probe 2’,7’-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (H2DCFDA). After the treatment with N-CPDs, cells were washed with PBS and subsequently stained with 1 μg/mL H2DCFDA for 30 min. The cells were washed twice with PBS, and the level of ROS was analyzed using a FACSCanto flow cytometer.

Mouse embryonic fibroblasts (NIH3T3) and human keratinocyte-forming cells (HaCaT) were inoculated in Petri dishes, cell suspensions were prepared, and cells were cultured until the appropriate number was reached in a cell culture incubator at 37 °C with 5 % CO₂. Subsequently, varying concentrations of N-CPDs were introduced to each well of the culture plate, which was then incubated for 24 h. The incubation period was continued for a further four hours. The optical density (OD) value of each well was determined at a wavelength of 450 nm using an enzyme marker, with the results subjected to statistical analysis and validation.

NIH3T3 cell scratch assay test was performed by 6-well plate selected and cultivated under conventional conditions until a cell monolayer was formed. On the cell monolayer, a 10-microliter gun tip was used to make a zigzag-shaped scratch along the bottom of the plate. The suspended cells were washed off twice with PBS and then added to serum-free culture medium to continue the incubation. The cells were observed and photographed after 24 h, and the relative distances of the scratched areas were recorded.

Fluorescence properties was measured with F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) at 220 eV was recorded in ESCALAB Xi+ spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) was recorded with AVATAR 360 FT-IR spectrophotometer, and KBr powder was employed as the sample matrix. The morphology and size of the N-CPDs were characterized using JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (TEM) at 200 kV. The crystal structure and phase analysis of the N-CPDs were characterized by smartlab X-ray diffractometer (XRD). The particle size of the N-CPDs was measured by Zetasizer Nano ZS nanoparticle size and zeta potential analyzer. All measurements were performed at room temperature under ambient conditions.

N-CPDs with blue fluorescence were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal synthesis reaction employing CA and PEI as raw materials. Carbon dots with high quantum yields are an essential parameter for the characterization of photoluminescence and even more critical for practical applications. The synthesis conditions were investigated using quantum yield (QY) and product yield (PY) as indicators, including the molar ratio of starting materials (CA:PEI, short for R), reaction temperature and reaction time. The quantum yield is calculated based on a rhodamine 6 G solution as a standard solution in this paper [26].

By comparing different experimental conditions, it is found that the R value has effect on the QY and PY of N-CPDs (Fig. S1 a). A very low R value results in a very low PY. Interestingly, the products with an excessively low R value exhibit very strong water absorption after freeze-dried and are extremely susceptible to moisture absorption and wetting when stored in air. The R is 3:1 can maximizes amide and C-N surface functionalities delivers the highest QY. In addition, the reaction temperature plays a key role in ensuring that the precursors undergo adequate dehydration and nucleation. At lower temperatures (140 °C), the precursors are not adequately prepared and surface modified, while higher reaction temperatures (220 °C) can lead to overcarbonization and thermal degradation of CA and PEI, thereby reducing the QY and PY values (Fig. S1 b). As for temperature, a reaction temperature of 180 °C can not only effectively promote rapid dehydration of citric acid leads to nucleation of small carbon cores, while PEI-mediated amidation concurrently attaches amino-rich passivation layers. The QY and PY of the products are at a higher level. As shown in (Fig. S1 c), the reaction time is another important factor that limits the QY and PY of N-CPDs. As the reaction time increases, the color of the product gradually changes from yellow to dark brown, and a long reaction time may lead to excessive carbonization. The results show that the reaction time of 3 h provides sufficient energy input for both carbon core maturation and PEI surface grafting.

By contrasting the group data, we take N-CPDs obtained at 180 °C for 3 h as a model, for a high degree of crosslinking occurs simultaneously with carbonization, and the amino group reacts with the carboxyl group to form a large number of amide bonds, with QY up to 72 % and PY up to 52 %. The quantum yields of most different types of nitrogen-doped carbon dots are lower than 50 %. The N-CPDs is higher than most reported QYs of doped carbon dots [27–29]. Also indicates that non-conjugated linear polymers or small molecules can be used to prepare cross-linked enhanced emission to obtain high QY [30]. The reaction product is a brown solid powder, and the reaction phenomena of different batches under the same synthetic conditions are consistent, which demonstrates that the synthesis is stable and reliable.

The N-CPDs powder was dispersed in ethanol, and then deposited onto an ultra-thin carbon film and allowed to dry. The size and morphology of the N-CPDs were revealed using high-resolution TEM. As shown in Fig. 1a, the N-CPDs are spherical in shape and exhibit a well-distributed morphology. And obvious lattice fringes at 2.2 nm can be observed in Fig. S2. As illustrated in the TEM insert of Fig. 1b, the dimensions of the N-CPDs are clearly visible. The size distribution is narrow, spanning a range of 2.0–3.2 nm with an average size of approximately 2.7 nm and a uniform distribution. This is consistent with its Malvern particle size shown in Fig. S3 [31] The UV-Vis spectrum exhibits a pronounced absorption peak at 350 nm in Fig. 1c, which is analogous to that observed in previously reported N-CPDs. The absorption peak is attributed to a π-π transition of the CO bond, indicative of the presence of a carbonyl or conjugated carbonyl group. The fluorescence intensity was quantified using a fluorescence spectrophotometer, and a distinct fluorescence peak was observed at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, which is in consistence with previous literatures [32,33]. And full width at half maximum is 65 nm that fundamentally enhanced its application [34]. The prepared dilute solution of N-CPDs is yellowish under visible light and strongly blue fluorescent under a 365 nm UV flashlight. As shown in Fig. 1d, the XRD pattern exhibits a broad peak centered at 2θ = 22.98°, which is analogous to the graphite lattice spacing and is attributed to the highly disordered carbon atoms.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) is a valuable tool for characterizing N-CPDs and obtaining information about their surface functional groups and composition. As illustrated in Fig. 1e, the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H corresponded to the absorption bands observed in the range of 3100–3500 cm⁻¹. C-H corresponded to the absorption band between 826 and 2943 cm⁻¹, and the band between 1549 and 1648 cm⁻¹, which corresponds to the stretching vibration of CN and CO respectively, provides evidence of the presence of amide groups. The characteristic peaks at 1355, 1154, 1128, and 763 cm⁻¹ represent the bending vibration of the –C(O)-O group and the C-O bond of the saturated ester, which indicates that an esterification reaction may have occurred in the sample. The presence of a considerable number of hydrophilic groups serves to enhance the environmental compatibility of the product, thereby facilitating its utilisation in biochemical research [35].

The functional groups were subjected to further analysis through the XPS. Fig. 1f depicts the XPS spectrum of N-CPDs, which exhibits three prominent peaks at 285 eV, 399 eV, and 531 eV. These peaks are attributed to O1s, N1s, and C1s, respectively, with atomic percentages of 63 %, 23 %, and 14 %. Fig. 1h depicts the high-resolution C1s spectrum, which can be effectively divided into three units, exhibiting multiple peaks at 284.6, 285.9, 287.3, and 288.3 eV, corresponding to CC, C–N/C–O, and CO groups[36]. The deconvolution of the O1s spectrum reveals the presence of two distinct peaks at 530.8 and 539.8 eV, which are attributed to quinone CO and C–OH/C–O–C. The N1 s spectrum in Fig. 1i can be decomposed into two components at 398.3 and 399.5 eV, which correspond to graphitic N and pyridinic N. These spectrums validate the N-CPDs successfully doped with a large amount of N. The above data are in close agreement with the results of FTIR, which demonstrate that N-CPDs contain elements such as C, H, and O, with N having been successfully doped. The surface of N-CPDs contains numerous hydrophilic functional groups comprising N and O elements. These functional groups enhance the hydrophilicity and stability of N-CPDs in aqueous systems, while also significantly contributing to the improvement of photoluminescence properties.

The various characterization methods were mutually verified in terms of size, structure, surface functions and elemental chemical states. The TEM and particle size distribution map show a highly uniform spherical structure of 2.0–3.2 nm. This narrow and consistent size distribution provides a physical basis for the sharp 350 nm absorption peak observed in UV–VIS and fluorescence spectra and the strong blue light emission under 360 nm excitation. The uniform particle size reduces the optical scattering and non-radiative composite pathways. Thereby enhancing the π→π* transition and luminescence efficiency. Then, the broad diffraction peak of 22.98° in XRD demonstrates the microcrystalline structure of the carbon skeleton, which coincides with the lattice fringes of TEM at 2.2 nm. Together with the presence of abundant surface functional groups in FTIR and XPS, it indicates that PEI not only introduces the -NH₂/amide bond as an N source but also acts as a passivator. Therefore, PEI plays two pivotal roles in the hydration reaction process: initially, as a precursor for N doping, and subsequently, as a passivator for carbon dots.

The fluorescence spectrum of N-CPDs at different excitation wavelengths (280–430 nm) is shown in Fig. 2a. As the excitation wavelength increases from 280 nm to 430 nm, the emission peak redshifts from 439 nm to 477 nm. The maximum fluorescence emission was achieved when the excitation wavelength was increased to 340 nm, and the emission peak was located at 450 nm. The half-peak full width of the strongest fluorescence emission peak was approximately 65 nm, and the size distribution was narrow. When the excitation wavelength reaches 430 nm, there is almost no fluorescence. CA act as a precursor to dehydration and aggregation to form smaller spherical nuclei, and PEI undergoes a cross-linking reaction during this process to form spherical N-CPDs. The exterior surfaces of these spheres are rich in functional groups, including epoxides, hydroxyl groups, and carboxyl groups, which serve as surface defects. These defects provide additional opportunities for electron jumps, thereby resulting in fluorescence.The partial aromatic network, which includes N/O-doped sp2-domains, is formed during the carbonization of CA [37]. This network is a contributing factor to the emission characteristics. The influence of surface defects on the emission characteristics is attributed to their sensitivity to functional groups and size, which can lead to a narrow energy level and a red shift.

The stability of N-CPDs is crucial for their practical applications, therefore, we further studied the effects of various conditions on the fluorescence stability of N-CPDs, including xenon light irradiation, storage time, and pH. The effects of pH conditions on the fluorescence properties of N-CPDs demonstrated notable discrepancies in Fig. 2b. In extreme acidic and alkaline environments, the fluorescence intensity exhibited a pronounced decline, whereas at a near-neutral pH of 7.0, it reached its maximum. With the decrease of PH, the position of the peak occurs blue shift, which may be related to the formation of multiple hydrogen bonds in the alcohol [38]. These data indicate that N-CPDs exhibit optimal performance in neutral environments. Fluorescence comes from an inner core containing pH sensitivity [39,40]. The detailed results illustrates the alteration in fluorescence intensity of N-CPDs when pH is varied from 1 to 14. The results satisfy the normal distribution with an R2 value of 0.973, which is beneficial for the application of pH sensors (Fig. S4 b). Fig. 2c shows the storage of N-CPDs in solid form and aqueous solution, respectively. It was determined that N-CPDs demonstrated good stability under solid storage conditions, whereas a 15 % reduction in fluorescence intensity was observed in diluted aqueous solutions. Extended storage periods result in the formation of a considerable number of carboxyl and amino groups on the surface of N-CPDs, which could interact with water molecules, leading to hydration or dissociation of some of the surface functional groups. This process may result in the loss of some surface functionalities, consequently affecting the fluorescence properties of the carbon dots. We tested the fluorescence spectra under xenon lamp irradiation (Fig. S4a). As shown in Fig. 2d, the fluorescence intensity of N-CPDs remained about 95 % of the initial value after 120 min of continuous irradiation with a 450 W xenon lamp, which demonstrated the excellent anti-photo-bleaching performance of N-CPDs. And the stability of varying temperatures (Fig. S5a) and repeated freeze-thaw cycles (cycling 6 times between − 20 ℃ and room temperature) has also been verified in Fig. S5b.

Typically, the prepared N-CPDs exhibited aggregation-induced fluorescence bursting phenomenon, which was specifically described as a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity with increasing concentration of N-CPDs. The effect of concentration of N-CPDs was investigated with aqueous solutions of N-CPDs with concentrations of 1, 3, 5, 7, and 10 mg/mL. As shown in Fig. 3a–e, the intensity showed a significant decrease with the increase of N-CPDs concentration, but then its intensity showed an increase when the concentration of N-CPDs was 10 mg/mL. Due to the N-CPDs are well dispersed at low concentrations, and the fluorescence emission relies on independent luminescent centers. And as the concentration increases, the N-CPDs begin to aggregate and form localized aggregated states, leading to π-π stacking. This can correspond to the broadening of absorption peaks seen in absorption spectra or the redshift, as well as the aromatic sp² hybridized structures observed in carbon nanomaterials in FITR. This effect initially manifests itself as a decrease in fluorescence intensity. At high concentrations, a certain upward trend in the fluorescence intensity is due to the possible formation of specific aggregation state structures in the N-CPDs. In these aggregated states, the free rotation and vibration of the molecules are restricted, reducing the possibility of non-radiative jumps and thus increasing the fluorescence efficiency. This phenomenon is known as Aggregation Induced Enhancement (AIE) [41,42].

In addition, the solutions with different concentrations were normalized at the maximum excitation wavelength, from which the redshift phenomenon of the fluorescence peak was clearly observed in Fig. 3f. The fluorescence peak was shifted from 460 nm to 520 nm, with an amplitude growth of only 60 nm, and appeared to have a saturated growth state. The fluorescence emission from red to blue can be adjusted by diluting high concentrations of N-CPDs, effectively changing the distance between the particles. This concentration-dependent color tunability increases its multifaceted applications, such as LED. The reason for this concentration-dependent property is due to the fact that at higher concentrations, N-CPDs may form compact aggregates, but the structure of these aggregates is a highly ordered π-π stack, and the electrons and holes are more likely to be combined by non-radiative pathways, which makes the energy level structure stable and leads to the saturation of the redshift amplitude [5].

The characterization of different preparation stages of N-CPDs was conducted by TEM, XRD, and UV–vis spectroscopy to understand the formation mechanism. The TEM images at varying temperatures are presented in Fig. 4. At 120 °C, the particles can not be observed and only large-sized polymers are present, indicates the initial condensation of CA. As the temperature is increased up to 140 °C, the CA undergoes dehydration and an amidation reaction, which results in the formation of graphene nuclei and the self-assembly of nanosheet-mounted structures. As the temperature is increased further, PEI facilitates a greater degree of nitrogen doping. Small particles peel off from the edges of the lamellar structure. The high-resolution TEM images do not display any clear lattice fringes. At temperatures up to 180 °C, the nanoparticles exhibited a more uniform and smaller morphology. As the reaction time was extended, the seeds of N-CPDs became visible within the graphene skeleton, and uniform particles emerged at 3 h with the decomposition of hours graphene skeleton. However, following a reaction time of four hours, the nanoparticles exhibited a gradual increase in size, accompanied by the emergence of lattice streaks. The extended reaction time permitted the carbon dots to undergo complete growth, enabling extensive cross-linking and aggregation, which in turn facilitated the aggregation of particles into larger structures. The elevated temperature during the reaction also accelerated the polymerization reaction, resulting in accelerated particle growth. The formation of lattice streaks indicates that regions in the polymer network have begun to crystallize, potentially due to the structural arrangement of the polymer chains. These insights contribute to the understanding of the reaction kinetics and the microscopic mechanism of the chemical reaction, which is crucial for optimizing the preparation of N-CPDs [43].

Given the obvious contrast in chemical composition between CA and PEI, an X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted to ascertain the synthesis process. The spectrums can be indexed as PEI and CA (Fig. S6 a), respectively. At a low temperature of 120 °C, the XRD spectrum displays multiple sharp peaks, indicating high crystallinity and poor crosslinking between CA and PEI. At temperatures up to 160 °C, a new peak emerges at 26°, indicative of the formation of a graphene-like skeleton derived from the amidation reaction between PEI and the nanodots. This peak then diminishes and vanishes, while the peak attributed to CA diminishes and vanishes, approaching the amorphous state of PEI. The duration of the reaction period influences the successive decarboxylation and carbonization process. The graphene skeleton emerges and subsequently vanishes, giving rise to uniform nanoparticles of N-CPDs at temperatures up to 180 °C. At a reaction time of three hours, the peaks exhibited only minor changes in width. However, when the reaction time reached five hours, a new peak emerged, indicating that the reaction had undergone excessive carbonization.

The UV-Vis absorption spectra of the different stages were subjected to analysis (Fig. S7). At low reaction temperatures, no significant change in absorbance was observed. With the temperature reaching 180℃, an absorption peak at 360 nm was generated, which can be attributed to the n-π transition of CN and C-N. Upon reaching a temperature of 200 °C, an absorption peak at 210 nm is observed, indicative of a π-π transition. The images demonstrated that the peaks in the absorption spectra remained at the same position at 2 h versus 3 h, although the peak maxima differed. As the reaction time is extended, the solution color darkens and the concentration of carbon nuclei increases, leading to the formation of more effective fluorescent nanodots. The optical properties and XRD images are in agreement, indicating that PEI affects the crystal structure, potentially by disrupting the formation of a regular lattice. Therefore, it is essential to control the reaction time to achieve optimal results. As shown in Fig. S8, according to the FTIR images, at reaction conditions of 180 °C for 3 h, the reaction tended to be complete, a cross-linked network was formed, the amide peak was strengthened, and the carboxylic acid was weakened.

During the reaction process, the effects of the molar ratio of CA:PEI, reaction time and reaction temperature on the particle size and quantum yield of the product were investigated respectively. The results showed that the particle size was the most uniform (2.7 ± 0.3 nm) under the conditions of a molar ratio of 3:1, 180 ℃ and 3 h, and the quantum yield was the highest (72 %). The reasons for high QY come from multiple aspects. Firstly, as a passivator, PEI provides a new channel for n-π* transitions through the formation of a large amount of primary amines, secondary amines and graphite nitrogen, significantly enhancing the probability of radiative transitions. Secondly, the N-PCDs generated by the hydrothermal method have uniform and small particle sizes and good dispersion by precisely controlling synthesis conditions (temperature, reaction time, and molar ratio of precursors.

In summary, it can be reasonably assumed that the synthesis of N-CPDs involves dehydration condensation, cross-linked polymerization, and carbonization. Initially, the CA-dominant and a partly PEI molecules dehydrate crosslinking through an intermolecular condensation reaction, thereby forming a graphene sheet structure and next becomes close-knit polymer structure core. As the reaction temperature increases, the highly nitrogen-containing PEI and CA carboxyl groups react completely, forming a large number of amide bonds and producing many tiny nanodots. Concurrently, the graphene backbone undergoes depletion. In this process, due to the high molecular structure and high degree of branching of PEI, not only does it facilitate the self-assembly of the nanodots, but it also stabilizes the formation of the graphene-like fluorescent structure through electrostatic and hydrogen bond interactions. At elevated temperatures and extended reaction times, the carbonization process is further carried out, and the amino groups of PEI promote additional nitrogen doping of the N-CPDs, increasing the nitrogen doping level of the N-CPDs, thereby forming stable N-CPDs.

The high QY renders N-CPDs a promising candidate for use as fluorescent probes for metal ion detection. As shown in Fig. 5, among the metal ions tested, Fe³⁺ exhibited the most pronounced bursting effect, attributable to its high redox potential, high number of unpaired electrons, and robust coordination ability [44]. In comparison, other metal ions, including K⁺, Na⁺, and NH₄⁺, which lack unpaired electrons and are predominantly electrostatic, demonstrated the least significant impact on fluorescence bursting. Under identical concentration and parallel testing conditions, N-CPDs demonstrated superior performance to other metal ions, exhibiting exceptional selectivity for Fe³⁺.

Fe³⁺ is a pivotal trace element within the human body, participating in a multitude of physiological processes such as oxygen transport, deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) synthesis, and cellular respiration. Additionally, it plays a crucial role in industrial applications and environmental testing. Development of carbon dots-based fluorescent sensors for Fe³⁺ offer a simple and rapid tool with the advantages of high sensitivity, low cost, and non-invasive sampling The aforementioned study demonstrated that the prepared N-CPDs exhibited heightened sensitivity to Fe³⁺ in comparison to other metal ions, such as Cu²⁺. Meanwhile through competitive-interference experiments, demonstrating minimal signal loss in the presence of excess non-target ions. A solution of Fe³⁺ was prepared at the same concentration as N-CPDs solution, with a concentration gradient ranging from 1 to 100 μM, to observe the change in fluorescence intensity [45]. Fig. 5 shows the fluorescence burst of N-CPDs after the addition of various concentrations of Fe³⁺. At the same excitation wavelength, the fluorescence intensity of N-CPDs exhibited a gradual decrease with the increase of Fe³⁺ concentration. Fig. S9 shows fluorescence images under different ion concentrations of Fe3+. No shift in the fluorescence emission position was observed. Fig. 5b inset revealed that the fluorescence intensity in the range of 1–100 μmol·L−1 exhibited a linear relationship with the concentration of Fe3+ with a high degree of correlation, and the corresponding regression coefficient was 0.99. F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity of N-CPDs in the absence and presence of interference. Compared with the original N-CPDs solution, the absorbance peak at 300–380 nm with the addition of Fe3+ gradually increases in intensity with the increase of metal concentration in Fig. S10. The increase in absorbance is not linear. The upward deviation of the curve from the straight line, especially at higher concentrations of Fe3+, along with a slight redshift in the ultraviolet-visible light absorption peak, inner filter effect (IFE) and static quenching may be the main factors causing the quenching effect [46]. Thus, the N-CPDs are highly suitable for the detection of Fe³⁺ due to their rapid and convenient detection of Fe³⁺ and the extensive measurement range of up to 100 μmol·L−1. This makes them a valuable tool for a wide range of Fe³⁺. The excellent photothermal stability and anti-photobleaching ability ensure the reliability of this platform in field and on-site detection. N-CPDs has a high selectivity for Fe3+ and has a minimal influence on interfering ions, making it suitable for food safety testing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of antibacterial and cytotoxicity of N-CPDs
ce:section.ce:para: Escherichia coli are typically gram-negative bacteria, while Staphylococcus aureus are typically gram-positive bacteria. These two types of bacteria are quite different in nature and are often used as typical indicator species for broad-spectrum antibacterial evaluation. By testing the inhibition rate of these two types of bacteria, an initial determination can be made as to whether the carbon dots materials have broad-spectrum antibacterial capability, which can provide a reference for subsequent expansion to other pathogenic bacteria. E. coli and Staphylococcus aureus were co-cultured with N-CPDs in LB broth, and then the solution was spread evenly on agar plates, and the bacterial colonies were photographed and counted to evaluate the antimicrobial effect after 24 h of incubation. As shown in Fig. 6, as the concentration of N-CPDs increased, the number of colonies on the petri dish decreased significantly. The antimicrobial effect was pronounced at N-CPDs up to 60 μg/mL and significantly fewer bacterial colonies were observed compared to the control. At a concentration of 100 μg/mL, the antimicrobial rate was close to 100 % in both dark and light, confirming that N-CPDs could effectively eliminate E. coli and S. aureus infections. Carbon dots possess a variety of antibacterial mechanisms, including physical/mechanical destruction, oxidative stress, and photocatalysis, etc [47,48]. The precise antibacterial mechanism of N-CPDs remains to be elucidated through further study. In addition, ROS was also found elevated in N-CPDs treated S. aureus and E. coli in a significance manner (Fig. 6f,h), suggesting that the ROS induced by N-CPDs may also contribute to bacterial inhibition. It can be known from the SEM images in Fig. S11 that N-CPDs has caused considerable damage to the cell membranes of bacteria [49,50]. We also verified the A. faecalis antibacterial property of N-CPDs (Fig. S12). Excellent optical properties and biocompatibility, provide ideal materials for multifunctional biosensors.

We further studied ROS production of N-CPDs conducted by measuring the electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) of the samples. The EPR measures the ROS by DMPO (spin trap) [51]. The spin trap detects superoxide and hydroxyl radicals of HO-DMPO resulting in a distinct quartet peak with 1:2:2:1 signal intensity for OH radicals shown in Fig. S13. Compared with the blank control group, the reactive oxygen species in N-CPDs increased significantly. The EPR data directly proved that N-CPDs can induce ROS production and induce ROS when exposed to bacteria, achieving an antibacterial effect [52]. The images in Fig. S13 show that the structures of S. aureus and E. coli observed in the figure are damaged, and the permeability of the bacterial membrane is significantly increased, supporting ROS-mediated membrane rupture and content outflow.

Low cytotoxicity and good cytocompatibility are essential for biological applications. Therefore, the cytocompatibility of N-CPDs was examined through the utilization of the CCK-8 assay on mouse embryonic fibroblasts (NIH3T3) and human keratinocytes (HaCaT). N-CPDs exhibited minimal impact on cell proliferation rate, even at a high concentration of 300 ng/mL, when contrast to the control group in Fig. 7. N-CPDs hydrodynamic radii less than 6 nm facilitate bodily excretion via renal avenues. Recently, it has been shown that enzymes found within the body have the capacity to “eat-away” at the carbon core of CDs and facilitate their biodegradation [53,54]. Subsequently, a cell scratch assay was used to simulate in vitro wound healing and to detect the rate of effect of N-CPDs on wound healing, thereby testing the ability of cells to migrate in two-dimensional space. The wound closure area of NIH3T3 cells treated with N-CPDs was 55.41 ± 3.17 %, while that of the control group was 57.83 ± 1.04 % in Fig. 7e, which was not significantly different from that of the control group. Based on the results of cell migration and cell compatibility studies, this further supports the potential application of N-CPDs in the biological field. N-CPDs exhibits excellent safety without obvious toxicity at the cellular level. Its water solubility and good stability enable it to be freely dispersed in the humoral environment without the need for additional carriers [55,56]. In this work, N-CPDs still maintained a cell survival rate of > 90 % at 300 μg/mL, which was comparable to that of highly biocompatible carbon-based nanoparticles, and significantly superior to metal nanoparticles and silica nanoparticles. Its toxicity level is also similar to that of commonly used polymer nanocarriers such as PLGA, further proving the superiority of N-CPDs in biomedical applications [57–59].

In summary, N-CPDs was successfully synthesized by hydrothermal synthesis with a quantum yield of up to 72 %, which is higher than the general level of carbon dots. The high QY and high sensitivity for F³⁺ of the prepared N-CPDs are expected to be applied for Fe³⁺ selective detection. By characterizing and analyzing the different preparation stages, we put forth potential formation process mechanisms, including dehydration condensation, cross-linked polymerization, and carbonization of polymer dots. The high antibacterial activity of N-CPDs can effectively inhibit bacterial proliferation, and its antibacterial mechanism will be further elucidated in future studies. Meanwhile, its low toxicity was also verified. Overall, this study may open a new avenue for the development of effective antibacterial drugs.
